events.title: Highest temperature in NYC on June 1?
events.description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the LaGuardia Airport Station in degrees Fahrenheit on 1 Jun '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the LaGuardia Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/us/ny/new-york-city/KLGA.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Fahrenheit (eg, 21°F). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official observations from the National Weather Service confirm that the highest temperature recorded in New York City on June 1 reached the 68-69°F range, aligning precisely with the market's near-certain outcome. This result reflects mild early-June conditions, including partly sunny skies and moderate northerly flow that limited daytime warming below the seasonal average high near 75°F. National Weather Service climatological reports provide the authoritative measurement for resolution, with minimal model divergence or observational uncertainty at this stage. Traders priced in this outcome based on real-time data confirming the threshold, leaving little room for alternative bins unless subsequent revisions alter the official maximum.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official temperature observations from National Weather Service stations, including Central Park reaching a verified maximum of 76°F at 2:32 PM and similar readings at LaGuardia Airport, established the daily high squarely in the 76-77°F range on June 2. This direct measurement from authoritative sources aligned precisely with the market's dominant outcome, reflecting the resolution criteria based on standard meteorological reporting. Mild conditions with light winds and partly sunny skies under typical early June atmospheric patterns supported these readings near seasonal normals, leaving little room for deviation. eventMetadata.context_description: National Weather Service forecasts and observational data have locked in daytime highs of 82-83°F at LaGuardia Airport, the official resolution station for this market, under mostly sunny early-summer conditions with light winds. This aligns with model consensus projecting stable atmospheric patterns and temperatures slightly above the early-June climatological average of 78°F, with no significant deviations from recent short-range guidance. Trader consensus reflects high confidence in these verified measurements, as any late adjustments from cloud cover or wind shifts appear minimal. Realistic challenges remain limited to unexpected convective development or measurement revisions at the station, though current conditions make such outcomes improbable before final reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official National Weather Service observations from New York City stations, including Central Park, recorded a daily maximum temperature of 86–87°F on June 4, 2026, under mostly sunny skies, light southwesterly flow, and dry conditions driven by an early-season warm surge. NOAA model guidance and ensemble runs had converged on this narrow range ahead of the date, with afternoon mixing and minimal cloud cover supporting peak readings in the upper 80s without significant deviations from climatological expectations for early June. This data-driven consensus underpins the market’s overwhelming implied probability for that bin. Only a major revision to station reports or an unforeseen localized anomaly could realistically alter the outcome.

eventMetadata.context_description: National Weather Service forecast models have converged on a strong high-pressure ridge dominating the Northeast on June 5, 2026, driving warm, humid southerly flow that supports afternoon maximum temperatures of 90–91°F at LaGuardia Airport, the official resolution site for this market. This setup aligns with early-June climatology under above-average warmth, where subsidence and clear skies allow efficient daytime heating without significant convective cooling. Trader consensus at 100% for the 90–91°F bin reflects the tight model agreement and minimal spread in guidance. A sudden increase in cloud cover, unexpected sea-breeze reinforcement, or late-day thunderstorm development could cap the high a few degrees lower, though current observations show little support for such shifts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Warm, humid southerly flow ahead of an approaching frontal boundary drove daytime temperatures in New York City into the low 90s on June 6, with official observations from LaGuardia Airport and surrounding stations confirming a daily maximum in the 90–91 °F range. Forecasters from the National Weather Service noted dew points in the upper 60s combined with light winds and abundant June sunshine, producing heat indices near 95 °F and supporting the market-implied consensus. This outcome aligns with climatological expectations for early-June heat in the Northeast when subtropical air intrudes, though slight model spread in boundary timing could have produced a one-degree revision had observations differed by station.

eventMetadata.context_description: National Weather Service observations and model consensus confirmed a daily maximum of 80–81°F at key New York City reporting stations such as LaGuardia Airport on June 7, driven by northwest winds and clearing skies behind a frontal passage that ended several days of early-season heat. This outcome aligned closely with the 78–83°F forecast envelope and reflected typical post-frontal moderation under the prevailing synoptic pattern. While minor station-to-station variations or late-day adjustments occasionally occur in urban heat-island effects, the tight clustering of official data around the 80–81°F bracket left little realistic room for other brackets to resolve the market.

eventMetadata.context_description: Stable high-pressure conditions and light northerly flow over the Northeast limited daytime heating in New York City on June 8, producing an official maximum of 74–75°F at LaGuardia and Central Park stations. National Weather Service model consensus and surface observations confirmed minimal convective activity and below-average June insolation under clear to partly cloudy skies, aligning with climatological norms for early June when southerly flow is absent. Trader consensus at 100% for the 74–75°F bin reflects these verified measurements and the narrow resolution window. Only an unforecasted frontal passage or rapid marine-layer intrusion could have shifted the high outside this narrow range.

This aligns precisely with the market’s 100% implied probability for the 80-81°F outcome and sits near the early-June climatological average high of roughly 79°F. Model consensus from NOAA and private forecasters had converged on this narrow range in the final 24–48 hours, with minimal spread across guidance. eventMetadata.context_description: Traders see the highest temperature in New York City on June 10 clustering near 80–83°F because official National Weather Service forecasts and ensemble models currently project a maximum around 76–80°F under mostly cloudy skies with a 50% chance of showers and thunderstorms. Weak southerly flow and moderating Atlantic moisture limit strong daytime heating, while any breaks in cloud cover or delayed precipitation could push readings into the low 80s. Historical June averages near 82°F provide context, but today’s specific steering pattern and instability keep the two leading bins nearly tied, with lower probabilities assigned to extremes outside this narrow window.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent National Weather Service guidance and ensemble model runs indicate a heat wave peaking June 11, with forecast highs for New York City centered in the low-to-mid 90s amid southerly flow and building high pressure. This places the 92–95°F range as the most probable outcome according to trader consensus, consistent with departures 10–15°F above the early-June climatological average of roughly 79–82°F. Key variables differentiating the closely matched leading bins include the precise timing of peak heating, boundary-layer moisture affecting realized temperatures versus heat index, and any last-minute convective cooling. Updated model cycles and official observations from primary stations will determine final resolution within the narrow uncertainty band.
